---
title: "Mindful Meal Practice"
format: html
---
# Mindful Meal Practice

Mindful eating is a grounding skill that helps you slow down, regulate attention, and reconnect with your body. In recovery, this can counter autopilot behavior and improve awareness of hunger, fullness, and emotional cues.

Rushed or distracted eating can increase stress and reduce self-awareness. Mindful meals build the opposite pattern: intentional pacing, sensory awareness, and present-moment focus.

Mindful routine practices strengthen attention control, which is a key skill for staying alcohol-free. Repeatedly slowing down daily behaviors helps counter autopilot patterns linked to past use.

## Meal Practice
At one meal this week:
1. Put away screens.
2. Take three slow breaths before first bite.
3. Notice taste, texture, and pace.
4. Pause halfway and check in with hunger/fullness.
5. End by naming your mood before and after.

::: {.callout-caution}
### When risk is higher

If urges or stress rise quickly, use this sequence early rather than waiting.

Repeat this for three meals and record what you notice.

Early action usually prevents the cycle from becoming harder to interrupt.
:::
